sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Unconsciousness refers to a state where a patient cannot be roused or do not respond to external stimuli, including sound, touch or other activities. Clinical unconsciousness can range from Syncope to coma. It is often associated with the patient having a Glasgow Coma Scale of 8 or less or being unresponsive on the AVPU scale. The condition requires immediate medical attention to address the underlying causes and stabilise the patient.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Unarousable and unresponsive: No purposeful response to verbal or tactile stimuli; patient may only exhibit reflex movements
- Absent or abnormal protective reflexes: Such as gag, cough, or swallowing
- Varied respiratory patterns: Normal, irregular or absent breathing
- Altered vital signs: May include hypotension, tachycardia, bradycardia, low or high blood sugar depending on underlying cause
- Possible associated features: Seizures, trauma, incontinence, tongue biting, abnormal posturing, or evidence of drug/toxin exposure
- Patients that cannot be roused or do not respond appropriately to external stimuli due to:
  - Hypovolaemia
  - Dysrhythmia
  - Hypoxia
  - Hypo/hyperglycaemia (hypoglycaemia may present as a focal neurological deficit or coma, whilst hyperglycaemia can lead to complications that can also result in unconsciousness.)
  - Toxins / Overdose
  - Hypo/hyperthermia
  - Sepsis
  - CVA / brain injury / infection / malignancy

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Prioritise scene safety for responders and bystanders before approach, especially in confined spaces or where toxic, electrical, or environmental hazards may exist
- Obtain witness history, medication history, recent illness, substance exposure, and relevant comorbidities as early as practical
- Manage airway compromise as a priority and anticipate aspiration, hypoventilation, trauma, or mixed medical and toxic causes
- All patients with abnormal conscious state should have blood glucose checked early and other reversible causes considered immediately
- Common causes include hypoxia, shock, dysrhythmia, hypoglycaemia, toxic exposure, seizure, stroke, CNS infection, trauma, and sepsis

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey with spinal motion restriction only where indicated
- Control catastrophic haemorrhage if present
- Open, clear, and maintain the airway, including suction and basic adjuncts where required
- Place laterally if unresponsive unless trauma or another condition requires a different position
- Provide oxygen and assist ventilation if required, paying attention to tidal volume and respiratory effort
- Record a full vital sign survey, particularly GCS, blood glucose, oxygen saturation, pupils, and temperature
- Perform focused secondary survey for trauma, toxic exposure, infection, seizure activity, or stroke features
- Keep the patient warm or initiate cooling where appropriate and seek senior support early

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway management if protective reflexes are absent or ventilation is inadequate
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain a 12 lead ECG when a cardiovascular, toxic, or metabolic cause is possible
- Establish vascular access if medication, fluids, or broader resuscitation may be required
- Consider point-of-care blood testing where available and clinically useful without delaying transport
- Manage identified abnormalities such as toxin exposure, dysrhythmia, shock, or seizure in line with the relevant condition-specific guideline
- Consider IV fluids if hypovolaemia, sepsis, or shock is suspected

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Some pre-hospital procedures such as injections may precipitate dizziness in susceptible patients. Patient position should be considered to avoid potential injury
- Unconsciousness of unknown cause should be treated as a high-risk undifferentiated emergency until reversible causes and major neurological, toxicological, cardiovascular, and traumatic causes have been considered
- Beware of QTc intervals greater than 460 msec in polypharmacy overdose and TBI; this can be an early warning sign of dangerous dysrhythmia
- See AEIOUTIPS for more information on possible causes
